Re-run daily digests for a targeted list of trips.

Designed for outage recovery: when the background digest loop logs

for the next 6-hour cycle.

Each trip's ``day_number`` is computed from its ``start_date`` and
the target date.  Trips with no ``start_date``, or where the target
date falls outside ``[start_date, end_date]``, are skipped with
status ``no_date``.

The endpoint is idempotent — ``generate_daily_digest`` respects its
input-hash skip optimisation, so re-running a trip that already has
an up-to-date digest returns ``status="skipped"`` without billable LLM
calls.

post.description:
Approve every pending candidate in one shot.

Each candidate is processed with the same atomic claim-first pattern as
the individual approve endpoint, so concurrent calls are safe. Candidates
that fail composition are skipped (claim reverted); the caller gets a
partial result. Always uses the mock composer — running N sequential LLM
composes inside a single request risks the idle-timeout and produces
~60–120 s latency; the individual approve path uses the LLM.

Backend validates each candidate's invariants (date order, photo count,
confidence tier matches stats) and persists. Rejects the whole batch
on any invalid candidate to keep client retry logic simple.

delete.description:
Mark a candidate as dismissed. Idempotent — repeated calls succeed silently.

Dismissed candidates do not re-appear in subsequent scans (the client
is expected to check the dismiss status before re-submitting candidates
that match a previously-dismissed date+place cluster).

Uses claim-first ordering (atomic UPDATE WHERE status='pending') to
eliminate the TOCTOU race from the old read-check-then-act pattern.

Delete an artifact and cascade-revoke its derived signals.

Triggers ``refresh_personal_memory()`` afterward so the next Travel DNA
synthesis sees the reduced signal set. Eventual-consistency note: the
LLM aggregate may preserve a signal it can no longer justify; the
dispute mechanism remains the user-facing fallback for stuck signals.
See the V0 plan's Signal extraction section.

get.description:
SSE stream of artifact generation events.

Two-phase: a ``shell`` event first (title / hero / map / placeholders
so the UI has something to render immediately), then a ``ready`` event
with the full artifact. True section-by-section streaming (yielding
sections as the LLM produces them) is V1 work — it needs the composer
to switch to ``stream_text`` and parse JSON incrementally. Today the
composer is single-shot, so this endpoint reads from the persisted
artifact and emits the two-phase shape for UI parity.

post.description:
Send a message and stream the response via Server-Sent Events.

Streams text deltas as they arrive from the LLM, then sends a final
event with metadata (tool calls, token counts, etc.).

If ``trigger_agent`` is False, returns a single SSE event with the
persisted message ID (no streaming needed).

Event types:
    - ``text_delta``: partial text chunk (``{"text": "..."}``).
    - ``metadata``: final turn metadata, sent once after the reply completes.
    - ``user_message``: when trigger_agent=False, the persisted message.
    - ``error``: if an exception occurs during processing.

Stamp the conversation's ``last_location`` with a fresh reading.

Participants only — the conversation's GPS is private to its
members. Mobile clients piggyback location on message sends for
the common path; this endpoint exists for background pushes
(foreground event, significant-change updates) where there's no
message to attach to.

Idempotent-ish: latest write wins. Readings older than 15 minutes
are filtered at read time by the origin resolver, so stale pushes
don't pollute — but callers shouldn't push stale readings anyway.

Promote a pre-trip conversation into a committed trip.

Reads the conversation's ``intent_state`` scratchpad, creates a trip
row with best-effort field mapping (title / place_id / dates),
links them both ways, copies conversation participants as trip
members, and stamps ``intent_state.phase='committed'``.

Only the conversation creator may promote — the resulting trip is
owned by that user (``trip.created_by = conversation.created_by``)
so allowing anyone else to drive this would let an attacker spawn
trips under a victim's name. Normal participant auth is not strong
enough here because trip creation is an ownership-changing action.

Fails with:
- 403 if the caller isn't the conversation creator
- 400 on not-found / home / already-promoted / not-ready

post.description:
Log a single user event.

Called by the frontend when a user performs an action (vote, view,
edit, etc.). Events are read by async pipelines via cursors.

When ``event_type == 'discover_session_end'``, a background task fires to
synthesize the session's Discover signals into Personal Memory observations.
The ``context`` field should contain ``session_start`` (ISO-8601 string) so
the synthesizer knows how far back to look.

Log multiple user events at once.

Useful for batching UI events or replaying actions. If any event in the
batch is ``discover_session_end``, synthesis is triggered once (using the
earliest ``session_start`` found in that event's context).

Every event in the batch must belong to the caller — a single
cross-user row rejects the whole batch. Same rule applies to
cross-trip rows (audit pass 2026-05-18 P1): a forged ``trip_id``
on any one event rejects the whole batch atomically.

B7 audit fix: restricted to the original ``paid_by`` (creator) OR a
trip organizer. Previously any trip member could mutate any other
member's expense — financial fraud within the trip.

post.description:
Mark shares as settled for the given users.

B6 audit fix: settlement is now restricted to either:
  * The payer (``actor.id == expense.paid_by``) — confirming "yes,
    these members paid me back", OR
  * Self-declaration (``body.user_ids == [actor.id]``) — declaring
    "I paid the payer back for my share".

Previously any trip member could mark ANY other member's share as
settled, falsely zeroing out a debt and effectively defrauding the
payer (the settlement screen showed the debt as cleared; the payer
never got paid).

Settle multiple expense shares in one request.

The single-expense ``/expenses/{id}/settle`` endpoint is unchanged;
this batch path collapses what was N parallel POSTs in
``Travel App/app/trip-expenses/index.tsx`` (one per expense in a
payer column) into one round-trip. Authorization is enforced
per-item — the same payer-or-self-only gate as the single endpoint
runs for every batch member, so a malicious caller cannot piggyback
a "settle someone else's debt" on top of a legitimate item.

Behaviour: fail-fast on the first unauthorized or missing item; no
partial commits. On success the response carries every updated
expense in request order so the client can refresh its cache in one
pass.

get.description:
Compose the proactive ``Concierge attention`` feed for this trip.

- Up to one ``pending_decision`` card lifted from the top open

- pending_decision → ProposalReviewSheet (existing)

Returns ``status='restored'`` when a row was actually removed,

Writes through ``create_observation`` with ``source_mode='proactive'``
and ``category='general'`` so the row is distinguishable from
agent-captured observations downstream (the reflection / digest
pipeline already consumes observations; user-captured ones flow into
the same path).

Trip-member-gated. Observations are auto-shared inside shareable
categories (food, accessibility, etc.); ``general`` captures default
to private — the writer can later widen via the personal-memory
surface if they want the group to see.

Auth: only the user themselves can request a routing for their

post.description:
Speculative pre-render hint — fires LLM + TTS before geofence dwell expires.

Phase Voice-1 / Phase 2.4. Called by the client when the user is
approaching a known stop (within ~200 m and walking toward it).
The endpoint is a thin wrapper that:

1. Verifies the actor is a trip member (existing auth).
2. Verifies the requested stop is part of THIS trip (avoids
   arbitrary speculative renders for stops the user isn't on).
3. Checks if audio for this stop is already cached → no-op return.
4. Otherwise enqueues ``render_intros_for_user_trip`` for the
   actor (the trip-level fan-out is idempotent; the worker skips
   already-rendered stops).

Idempotent and racy-safe — multiple clients triggering the same
hint within the cache TTL all converge on the same cached audio,
so over-firing is cheap. The endpoint accepts a single
``(entity_type, entity_id)`` rather than a list to keep the client
simple; if the user is approaching multiple stops, the client
fires multiple hints in parallel.

get.description:
Return the offline-narration manifest for the actor on this trip.

Phase Voice-1 / Phase 2.5. The client fetches this once on WiFi
pre-departure, batch-downloads every audio entry to local storage,
and plays from the local path while offline / on expensive data.

Each ``audio_url`` is relative to the API base — clients construct
the absolute URL via their own configured BASE_URL. Re-fetching the
manifest after additional renders complete returns a superset of
the previous response (idempotent + monotonic).

The ``ready_count`` and ``pending_count`` let the client surface a
"ready for offline" indicator. When ``pending_count > 0``, the
client may choose to defer the offline-bundle download and re-poll
the manifest after a short delay.

head.description:
HEAD probe for pre-rendered narration audio — cheap existence check.

Returns 200 with ``Content-Length`` and ``Content-Type`` headers when audio
is cached and ready, 404 when not.

Why a dedicated HEAD route rather than relying on FastAPI/Starlette's
implicit HEAD-from-GET behaviour:
- The GET handler returns a ``StreamingResponse`` backed by a generator.
  Starlette's implicit HEAD strips the body but still executes the handler
  including the generator, meaning it fetches the full audio blob just to
  discard it.
- This explicit HEAD issues a single lightweight COUNT-style query against
  ``narration_audio_cache``, reading only ``audio_size_bytes`` (no blob).
- Matches the client contract: ``useNarrationAudio`` does a HEAD check
  to confirm availability before creating an ``expo-audio`` player;
  eliminating the GET overhead makes that check ~10× cheaper.

get.description:
Return pre-rendered MP3 audio for a stop, or 404 if not yet cached.

Used by ``useNarrationAudio`` on the mobile client to play narration audio
when available. The audio cache is populated by the prerender pipeline
(``backend/guide/prerender.py``) which fires on ``itinerary.committed``
events. Cache miss is normal — the chat narration card silently
omits the play button when audio isn't ready.

For a lightweight existence check (no blob transfer), use the sibling
``HEAD /audio/{entity_type}/{entity_id}`` endpoint.

post.description:
Force-recompose the Trip Story for the caller on this trip.

Enqueues a background composition job and returns 202 Accepted
immediately. The client should poll GET /api/trips/{trip_id}/story
until it receives a 200 with the full TripStory body.  Using a
background job prevents this POST from blocking for the 30–90s
that a Sonnet recomposition takes.

The job is deduped via ``_job_id`` so rapid taps never spawn more
than one concurrent recompose run per user.

202.description: Recomposition enqueued — poll GET story for result

get.description:
Return the composed Trip Story for the caller on this trip.

If no story exists yet, enqueues a background composition job and
returns **202 Accepted** with ``{"status": "composing"}``.  The
client should poll every few seconds until it receives a 200 with the
full TripStory body.  Using a background job (instead of an inline
``compose_trip_story`` call) keeps this GET from blocking for the
30–90s that a Sonnet composition takes on slow networks.

The job is deduped via ``_job_id=f"story:{trip_id}:{user_id}"`` so
a polling client never spawns more than one composition run per user.

Record a user's response to a notification.

``response_type`` advances the outcome's state forward only
(pending → seen → acknowledged → engaged → acted).
Sending a weaker type than the current state is a no-op — returns
204 regardless so the client never needs to know the current state.

Typically called:
- ``seen`` — user dismissed the banner / opened the conversation
- ``engaged`` — user tapped the notification / replied in the chat
- ``acted`` — user completed the suggested action (e.g. booked a venue)

Stream a personalized Take over SSE — prose appears as the model writes it.

Events (named to match the shared SSE client in the app's ``utils/sse.ts``
so it reuses that hardened reader unchanged):
  - ``text_delta``: ``{"text": "<raw chunk>"}`` — raw model output as it streams.
  - ``metadata``:   ``{"take": <EntityTake>, "was_cached": bool, "tier": "personal"}``
    — the authoritative parsed Take. Also emitted (with no deltas) on a cache
    hit or a generation fallback.

Streaming is only for *personalized* Takes (``user_id`` required) — the
Curator Take is pre-rendered and instant, so fetch it via ``POST /api/takes``.
Auth / rate-limit / context assembly mirror the sync endpoint; only the
generation step differs (a token stream instead of one JSON return).

patch.description:
Update user-editable trip settings.

Any combination of ``title``, ``place_id``, ``start_date``, ``end_date``,
``occasion``, ``budget_band``, and ``voting_enabled`` may be updated.
Omitted fields are left unchanged (standard PATCH semantics — only fields
present in the request body are applied).

Only trip organizers may call this endpoint. Members who want to propose
changes should do so through the concierge conversation.

Internal fields (``status``, ``trip_summary``, ``planning_brief``) are
managed by background jobs and the concierge, and are not accepted here.

Side effect: when fields that shape cross-trip threading change
(``title``, ``start_date``, ``end_date``, ``occasion``, ``budget_band``),
schedule a background recompute of ``trip_cross_trip_threads`` so the
trip-home surface reflects the new context on next read.

get.description:
Return the cached cross-trip threads for ``trip_id``.

Each thread carries a short concierge-voice prose string plus 1-2
references to prior trips (hydrated with title at read time so the
frontend doesn't need a second fetch per chip).

Behaviour:
- Cache miss → schedule background recompute, return empty threads.
- Cache stale (source_trip_count < current completed-trip count for
  this user) → schedule background recompute, return cached threads
  so the UI doesn't flicker.
- Cache fresh → return cached threads directly.

No threads is the canonical state for users with < 2 prior completed
trips. The frontend hides the card when threads is empty.

post.description:
Create a new trip in ideation status.

The creator is automatically added as an organizer so they have
authority to finalize proposals, edit trip shape, etc.

**Blank-trip dedupe (2026-05-27 overhaul):** when the request has no
title, destination, place_id, dates, occasion, or budget_band — i.e.
the user tapped "+ New trip" with no intent yet — the route returns
the user's existing blank trip if one exists, rather than creating
a second placeholder. This enforces the "one draft slot per user"
product rule and prevents the empty-trip clutter that motivated the
overhaul. Requests with ANY substantive field (destination, dates,
etc.) always create a new trip — distinct intent = distinct trip.

Pass ``X-Idempotency-Key`` to prevent duplicate trips on a slow-
network retry. Audit pass 2026-05-19 P0: pre-fix the route read
no header and a flaky-network retry created a second trip even
though the FE had been threading the key since the prior audit.

get.description:
Return the caller's blank trip, or 404 if none exists.

Used by the frontend to (a) detect whether a "Draft trip" chip
should render on the Trips tab, (b) route ``+ New trip`` to the
existing draft slot rather than creating a duplicate. See
``get_blank_trip_for_user`` for the matching definition of "blank".

post.description:
Archive a trip and return its conversation to drafting.

The two-way door from promotion. Trip row stays (status='archived')
for audit / undo. The primary conversation is unlinked and its
intent_state.phase set back to 'drafting' so the agent resumes
exploration without losing history.

Only trip members can decommit. The trip doesn't have to be 'ideation' —
later statuses can also be decommitted (e.g. user changed their mind
after confirming).

Email the most recent daily digest for this trip to the caller.

On-demand only in this phase — we don't push digests on a
schedule because the cadence decision (daily? weekly? per-trip?)
is a product question we haven't resolved. This endpoint lets a
user pull their digest to their inbox whenever they want, so the
digest engine's output actually reaches them.

Gates:
- Actor must be a trip member (existing ``_require_trip_member``)
- Actor's ``email`` must be non-null (from Clerk)
- At least one daily digest must exist (the engine populates these
  via the ``_run_digest_loop`` background task)

post.description:
Move a block to a different day and/or time slot.

Handles cross-day moves atomically (single UPDATE on day_id + sort_order +

Pre-flight conflict check for the Move sheet's IF YOU MOVE IT section.

- ``travel_*_ok`` — soft travel-time warnings against adjacent blocks
- ``would_create`` — Phase 3 standing-watcher scan run against the target

Nothing is written to the DB. The frontend calls this debounced as the

Scans the block's current day for clear gaps sized to the block's own

Venue-backed: supply ``venue_id`` + ``block_type="venue"``.

The block lands at ``max(sort_order)+1`` on the target day.

Searches venues near the midpoint of the adjacent blocks' venues (or the

``BlockAlternativesResponse`` shape as ``/alternatives`` so the frontend

Scan all days for §7.2 planning conflicts (standing watcher).

Returns conflicts grouped by day. Clients call this on plan open and
after any itinerary edit. No edit-mode gate — read-only.

patch.description:
Set or clear the trip's daily / total budget.

Organizer-only. Passing ``null`` for a field clears it; omitting a field
leaves it untouched. (PATCH semantics — distinguished via
``model_fields_set`` so a request that only sets the daily budget does
not silently null out the total budget.) Amounts are in the smallest
currency unit (cents for USD/EUR).

Read-only preview — never commits. The client applies (when the user
accepts the preview) by issuing N ``move_block`` calls in the suggested
order, which goes through the existing direct-edit gate and Undo spine.

Member-only; no edit-mode gate since this is a read. The destination
move_block calls during Apply enforce the gate themselves.

Record that the acting user dismissed the leave-by nudge for a block.

Sets ``dismissed_at`` on the most-recent leave_by_dispatches row for

Mute all leave-by nudges for this user + trip for the rest of today.

Inserts a leave_by_mutes row keyed on (user_id, trip_id, date). The date
is taken from ``body.local_date`` when provided (the device's local calendar
date), falling back to today in UTC. Using the device date prevents timezone
skew for users ahead of UTC (e.g. UTC+9) who would otherwise mute the wrong
day.

Suppression rule 1 in the dispatcher checks this table before dispatching.
Idempotent — inserting the same mute row twice is a no-op (composite PK).

Aggregates: user row + latest personal memory taste dimensions +

Aggregates each trip's itinerary blocks that resolved to coordinates into

you've been, pins colored by trip). The per-trip equivalent is
``GET /api/trips/{id}/map-state``. Runs the sync aggregation off the

Writes an observation tagged with ``category="taste_dna_feedback"`` and

delete.description:
Permanently delete the user's account and all personal data.

Irreversible. Shared trips and group conversations the user created are
reassigned to remaining members (or deleted if the user was alone);
everything personal to the user — memory, messages, expenses, receipts,
photos, bookings, devices, notifications — is removed. See
``backend.core.db.account_deletion.delete_user_data`` for the full
cascade and the cooperatively-owned-data policy.

get.description:
Return the user's ambient home conversation, creating on miss.

The home conversation is the trip-less chat where the concierge
relationship lives between trips — inspiration, cross-trip questions,
late-night musings. One per user; bootstrapped at user creation.
This endpoint also backfills for any user who predates the bootstrap.

delete.description:
Erase everything the concierge has *learned* about the user.

Trips, messages, expenses, hard constraints, follows, and saves are
all preserved. Only synthesized + observed preference data is wiped.
See ``backend.core.db.traveler.reset_personal_data`` for the full
list of tables touched.

get.description: Return the authenticated user's latest Personal Memory document.

Return a copy of all data we hold about the user (right to access).

post.description:
Set or change the user's phone number.

Clears any prior verification state — changing the number requires
a fresh verification before SMS will be delivered. Pass
``phone_number=null`` to remove the number entirely.

Idempotency: a stable ``X-Idempotency-Key`` returns the cached
response (bad-network audit 2026-05-19). Without it, a retry races
the unique partial index and either returns ``409`` (number now
"in use" by the user's own re-write) or silently succeeds with a
different verification state than the original request observed.

Generate a fresh OTP and send it via SMS.

Subsequent calls within the 10-minute expiry invalidate the prior
code (fresh OTP). No rate limit beyond what Twilio enforces —
worth adding server-side throttling when real usage warrants.

Idempotency: critical here because a flaky-network retry on the
"Send code" button would otherwise burn a second SMS against the
user's per-number rate-limit budget (and produce a confusing UX
where two codes land seconds apart). The cache returns the prior
response (provider + sent flag) so the retry is a no-op.

Submit the OTP to mark the phone number as verified.

Idempotency: a retry of the same code submission returns the
cached response rather than burning a second attempt against the
user's rate-limit budget.

Use this when the concierge has recorded something wrong — e.g.,

If the fact is *outdated* rather than *wrong*, the concierge will

get.description: Return a one-line insight extracted from the user's Personal Memory.

Return a personalized list of venue recommendations.

Scoring pipeline:
  1. Collect published angles, filter excluded IDs, take top 30 by distinctiveness
  2. LLM personalization rerank (Haiku, angle_rank.py)
  3. Engagement stats from angle_user_engagement materialized view
  4. LLM cold prior for angles with < MIN_IMPRESSIONS_FOR_WARM impressions
  5. Three-way blend: 40% LLM + 40% engagement + 20% editorial
  6. Lens-diversity injection (max 2 consecutive same-lens)
  7. Return top 8

Events at step 8 use taste-DNA fan-out (experience_fan_out_search) ranked
by Personal Memory; fall back to recency ordering when fan-out returns empty.

``exclude_angle_ids`` — filtered before personalization to skip angles
already shown this session.

Record a geofence trigger and (if applicable) open a session.

Auto-session semantics:
  - On ``entered`` AND no active session → open one anchored at the
    triggering entity and bind the new event row to it via session_id.
  - On ``entered`` for a different entity → open a new session. The
    CRUD helper closes the prior open row with ``next_place`` first,
    preserving its arc summary/history while starting a fresh arc.
  - On any other event type, or when the entered entity matches the
    active session → record with the current session_id (when present).

The auto-open behavior keeps the client flow tight: a fresh geofence
enter → one HTTP call → client knows both the event id and the
session id. Saves the round-trip vs separate "create session" + "record event."

get.description: Basic liveness check — always returns 200 if the process is running.
Readiness check — verifies Postgres and Qdrant are reachable.

Monitoring-only check of external API reachability (B7).

Returns status of outbound dependencies the runtime relies on:
Anthropic (LLM), Tavily (web search), and Redis (the arq worker's
job intake). Used by dashboards / alerting — NOT wired into the
readiness probe because a transient blip in any of these shouldn't
make the pod un-routable (cached / non-LLM endpoints still serve,
and Redis-backed work falls back to inline execution). Redis lives
here rather than in /ready precisely because it is a background-only
dependency: a Redis outage degrades digests / audio prerender /
pre-trip jobs but must not take the synchronous chat path offline.

Behaviour:

- Skipped checks (no API key configured) report ``skipped``;
  not a failure.
- Reachable checks report ``ok``.
- Unreachable checks report ``error: <reason>`` but the response
  still returns 200 — degraded signal lives in the body, not the
  HTTP status. Alerting consumes the body.

The Anthropic check uses a cheap ``count_tokens`` API call when
available (no token billing); falls back to a TCP-connect probe
when the SDK doesn't expose count_tokens.
